reasoning:
The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index jumped sharply from 4.91 in late Feb to 6.31 on Apr 9, settling at 6.15 on Apr 11. Search results indicate this spike is driven by an ongoing global trade war, with EU/China retaliatory tariffs announced right around April 11-12. This sparked a massive sell-off in European equities and widening of credit spreads. Because this retaliatory phase of the trade war is highly current, momentum favors further spread widening over the very short term (the FRED statistical model estimates near-term continuation of the upward trend at P=0.62). Thus, the probability for April 20 remaining above the 6.15 threshold is elevated.

However, sharp 100+ bps yield spikes typically encounter mean reversion within a few months. Furthermore, the ECB holds a policy meeting on April 16-17. Faced with a growth-damaging trade shock, the ECB is likely to lean dovish, which could push the base-rate component of these yields down over the coming months. Therefore, while near-term panic supports higher yields, eventual central bank intervention and market stabilization favor mean reversion. To reflect increasing uncertainty over longer horizons, the probabilities smoothly converge toward 0.50 for the medium-to-long term resolution dates.
